_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against WHO Yaws Eradication Strategy 2020, Mitja Lancet 2015 azithromycin trial, Marks Lancet 2018 monitoring data, and CDC parasitic & tropical disease references.

h1Title: Yaws: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Yaws is a chronic skin and bone infection caused by Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue, a spirochete bacterium related to the syphilis pathogen but transmitted by direct skin contact rather than sexually. It almost exclusively affects children under 15 living in warm, humid, rural areas of West and Central Africa, the Pacific Islands, and Southeast Asia. The disease begins as a single raspberry-like papilloma at the site of skin contact and, if untreated, progresses over years to disfiguring bone and cartilage destruction. A single 30 mg/kg oral dose of azithromycin cures over 95% of cases, and the WHO has set 2030 as the target for global eradication. Yaws is one of only two human diseases targeted for eradication alongside polio.

keyStats.prevalence: ~84,000 cases reported globally in 2022; ~46,000 confirmed (WHO 2023). Endemic in 13 countries with surveillance.
keyStats.demographics: 75-80% of cases in children under 15; equal sex distribution
keyStats.avgAge: Primary lesion typically appears between ages 2 and 14, with peak incidence at 6-10 years
keyStats.globalCases: ~46,000 confirmed cases per year across 13 endemic countries; true burden estimated higher due to under-reporting

definition: Yaws (ICD-10: A66) is a non-venereal, chronic granulomatous infection of skin, cartilage, and bone caused by Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue. It is one of four endemic treponematoses alongside venereal syphilis (T. pallidum pallidum), bejel (T. pallidum endemicum), and pinta (T. carateum). Transmission is exclusively through direct skin-to-skin contact, usually through minor abrasions, and the disease is not sexually transmitted and not congenital. Clinically the infection unfolds in three stages. Primary yaws is a single papilloma — historically called the 'mother yaw' — appearing 9-90 days after exposure, typically on the legs or buttocks. Secondary yaws develops weeks to months later as multiple smaller papillomas, plantar hyperkeratosis, and bone pain. Tertiary yaws, now rare due to mass treatment campaigns, emerges 5-10 years after primary infection and causes destructive gummatous lesions, saber-tibia deformity, and disfiguring craniofacial damage termed gangosa. Yaws is managed by infectious disease physicians, dermatologists, and primary care clinicians in endemic settings, with WHO coordinating eradication through community-wide azithromycin distribution. The Morges Strategy, adopted in 2012, replaced injectable benzathine penicillin with oral azithromycin and is the operational backbone of the eradication plan.

typesClassification.description: Single papilloma (the mother yaw) at the inoculation site, appearing 9-90 days after contact. The lesion is painless, raspberry-like, 1-5 cm wide, and heals spontaneously over 3-6 months even without treatment.

Multiple smaller papillomas across the body, plus thickened painful soles (plantar hyperkeratosis), bone pain in the long bones, and lymphadenopathy. Develops weeks to months after the primary lesion.
Serologically positive but asymptomatic. Most untreated patients pass through prolonged latency. Relapses into infectious secondary disease can occur within 5 years.

Destructive gummatous lesions of skin, bone, and cartilage 5-10 years after primary infection. Includes saber tibia, juxta-articular nodes, and gangosa — destruction of the nose, palate, and pharynx. Now rare due to community-wide treatment.

primarySymptoms: A single painless raspberry-like papilloma (the mother yaw), 1-5 cm wide, appearing at the site of skin contact 9-90 days after exposure — most often on the leg, foot, or buttock.
Multiple smaller papillomas spreading across the trunk and limbs weeks to months later, sometimes coalescing into ulcerated plaques.
Painful thickening and cracking of the soles (plantar hyperkeratosis, called 'crab yaws'), making walking on the heels characteristic and the source of the historical name.
Deep bone pain in the long bones (tibia, ulna, fingers), worse at night, due to periostitis and osteitis from spirochete invasion.
Regional lymphadenopathy near the primary lesion, with rubbery non-tender nodes 1-2 cm in diameter.
Saber-shaped bowing of the tibia (saber tibia) developing over years of untreated infection due to chronic periosteal thickening.
Disfiguring destruction of the nose, palate, and central face called gangosa, occurring in late tertiary disease after 5-10 years untreated.
Juxta-articular subcutaneous nodes — firm rubbery nodules near elbows, knees, and other large joints — a hallmark of tertiary yaws.
Hyperpigmented or hypopigmented patches at the sites of healed lesions, often persisting for years.
General constitutional symptoms during secondary disease including malaise, low-grade fever, and headache.
earlyWarningSigns: A new, painless, slowly-growing papilloma on a child's leg or foot in a yaws-endemic area
Living in or recent travel to West/Central Africa, Pacific Islands, Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, or rural Indonesia
Multiple skin lesions appearing on a child after another household contact has had yaws
Painful soles and difficulty walking on flat ground in a school-age child in an endemic community
emergencySigns: Rapidly destructive facial lesions involving nose, mouth, or palate — late gangosa requires urgent referral and may indicate co-infection
Secondary bacterial infection of yaws ulcers causing fever, surrounding cellulitis, and rapid expansion — needs systemic antibiotics
Septic arthritis of a joint adjacent to a yaws lesion in a child — requires aspiration and culture
Severe Jarisch-Herxheimer-like reaction after azithromycin in a child with very high spirochete burden — supportive care needed

causes.cause: Infection with Treponema pallidum subsp. pertenue
causes.description: The spirochete bacterium responsible for yaws. Morphologically and genetically near-identical to T. pallidum pallidum (the syphilis pathogen), differing by approximately 0.2% at the genomic level, but biologically restricted to skin and bone in temperate-to-tropical climates.

causes.cause: Direct skin-to-skin transmission through minor abrasions
causes.description: The bacterium enters through small cuts, scratches, insect bites, or eczema. Children playing in close contact in endemic communities transmit it efficiently. There is no sexual or congenital transmission.

causes.cause: Warm, humid climate above 27°C average temperature
causes.description: T. pertenue requires sustained skin moisture to survive briefly outside the host. Endemic transmission is confined to tropical lowland regions with high humidity and rainfall above 1,250 mm annually.

causes.cause: Crowded living conditions with limited access to water and soap
causes.description: Communities without reliable handwashing facilities, where children share bedding and play closely, have much higher transmission. Improved hygiene alone reduces incidence by 30-50% even without antibiotic mass treatment.
A single untreated person with secondary lesions can infect multiple children in their household. This is why WHO eradication strategy targets community-wide treatment rather than individual cases.

riskFactors.description: Children in this age band account for 75-80% of cases due to closer skin contact during play, more frequent skin breaks, and lack of acquired immunity from prior exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Living in a tropical endemic region
riskFactors.description: West and Central Africa, Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, rural Indonesia, and parts of Southeast Asia carry essentially all current cases. Travel-acquired yaws is rare.

riskFactors.factor: Poverty and lack of access to soap and clean water
riskFactors.description: Communities without reliable hygiene supplies have 3-5 fold higher attack rates. Improved water access measurably reduces incidence.

riskFactors.factor: Crowded household contacts with active cases
riskFactors.description: Household contacts of an active case have a 10-20% probability of acquiring the infection over the following 6 months without intervention.

riskFactors.factor: Skin abrasions, eczema, or insect bites
riskFactors.description: Any break in the skin barrier facilitates entry. Children with chronic skin conditions or frequent minor injuries from playing barefoot are at elevated risk.
Some studies report a slight male predominance (~1.2-1.5x) likely reflecting outdoor activity patterns rather than biological susceptibility.

affectedDemographics: 13 currently endemic countries reported cases to WHO in the 2018-2022 period
Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands together account for over 70% of reported cases globally
Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Republic of the Congo, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo contribute the majority of African cases
Children under 15 account for 75-80% of new infections in active outbreaks
Tertiary yaws is now extremely rare in surveillance data — most affected adults reflect childhood untreated infections from the 1950s-1970s
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical recognition of a characteristic lesion in an endemic-area child with serologic confirmation. The WHO operational case definition for surveillance is: a person living in an endemic area with one or more typical papilloma or ulcer plus a positive rapid treponemal test. Direct dark-field microscopy of papilloma fluid can show motile spirochetes, but the test is logistically difficult in field settings. Serology is the practical mainstay. Two complementary tests are used: a treponemal test (rapid TPHA or DPP Syphilis Screen and Confirm) detects antibodies that persist for life and confirms exposure, and a non-treponemal test (RPR or VDRL) measures active disease and reverts to negative or low titer after successful treatment. A fourfold drop in RPR titer at 6 months is the cure marker. PCR testing for T. pallidum DNA on lesion swabs is the most specific tool and is the reference method in WHO eradication trials — it can also detect the macrolide-resistance 23S rRNA mutation that emerged after a 2018 outbreak in Papua New Guinea. Skeletal X-ray shows periosteal thickening of the tibia and ulna in advanced disease. The key clinical distinction is from venereal syphilis, which is serologically indistinguishable. In a child under 15 in an endemic area with no sexual history, positive treponemal serology essentially confirms yaws.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid treponemal test (DPP Syphilis Screen and Confirm)
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line field test that detects both treponemal and non-treponemal antibodies on one cassette from a finger-prick sample. Used by WHO for mass screening during eradication campaigns.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A drop of finger-prick blood placed on a test card. Results read in 15-20 minutes. No fasting or preparation needed.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid Plasma Reagin (RPR) or VDRL titer
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies active infection and tracks treatment response. A fourfold (two-tube) drop in titer at 6 months post-treatment is the cure criterion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw sent to a regional lab. Results return in 1-3 days. Repeat testing is the key monitoring step at 6 and 12 months.

diagnosticTests.test: T. pallidum Particle Agglutination (TPPA) or Treponema pallidum Hemagglutination (TPHA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirmatory treponemal test that distinguishes treponemal infection from false-positive RPR. Remains positive for life regardless of treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood test. Results in 1-3 days. Useful for case classification but cannot distinguish active from past infection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates motile spirochetes from active papillomata under specialized lighting. Highly specific but requires a fresh sample and trained operator.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician squeezes a small amount of fluid from the lesion onto a glass slide and examines it under a dark-field microscope within minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard for confirmation and the only test that can detect the 23S rRNA macrolide-resistance mutation. Used in WHO surveillance and outbreak investigations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Swab of a moist papilloma sent in transport medium to a reference laboratory. Results in 1-2 weeks depending on logistics.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies periosteal thickening (saber tibia), osteitis of fingers and ulnae, and gummatous bone destruction in tertiary disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard two-view radiograph of the affected bones, typically tibia or forearm. A few minutes per limb; results read same day in most centers.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is the centerpiece of WHO's eradication strategy and is now built around a single oral dose of azithromycin 30 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) for both individual cases and household or community contacts. This shift was made possible by the landmark trial of Mitja and colleagues in Papua New Guinea (Lancet 2012), which showed azithromycin was non-inferior to the prior gold-standard of intramuscular benzathine penicillin and could be delivered at community scale without injections. Cure rates of 95-97% are documented when used in mass drug administration campaigns. Benzathine benzylpenicillin 1.2 million units intramuscularly (600,000 units in children under 10) remains the recommended alternative for patients with azithromycin allergy, suspected macrolide resistance, or pregnancy where azithromycin is preferred but penicillin is acceptable. The WHO Morges Strategy involves total community treatment of every resident regardless of symptom status, followed by total targeted treatment of any new cases and their contacts at 6-month intervals until the prevalence is below 1 per 10,000 for at least 3 consecutive years. Monitoring relies on RPR titer fall at 6 months; failure of a fourfold titer drop should prompt re-treatment and PCR-based resistance testing. The 2018 Papua New Guinea outbreak of azithromycin-resistant yaws — caused by an A2058G mutation in 23S rRNA — has driven careful surveillance, and penicillin remains universally effective against resistant strains. Surgical reconstruction is rarely needed in modern practice; gangosa and saber-tibia deformities are residual scars from pre-eradication-era untreated infections. Children with painful plantar hyperkeratosis benefit from topical keratolytics and soft padded footwear during the weeks after antibiotic treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (single oral dose 30 mg/kg, maximum 2 g)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for both individual cases and mass community administration. WHO Morges Strategy backbone since 2012. Logistically far easier than injectable penicillin and is delivered by community health workers without requiring sterile equipment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 95-97% at 6 months in randomized trials (Mitja 2012, 2015); titer fall in over 90% of treated cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Benzathine benzylpenicillin (1.2 million units IM in adults, 600,000 units IM in children under 10)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative first-line, especially where macrolide resistance is suspected, in pregnancy, or after azithromycin failure. Single intramuscular injection. Effective against all currently known T. pertenue strains including azithromycin-resistant variants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate exceeds 95% in pre-eradication-era field studies; remains 100% effective in vitro against resistant strains.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for patients allergic to both azithromycin and penicillin. Not for children under 8 due to dental staining. Adherence over 14 days is a logistic challenge in field settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in case reports and small series; no head-to-head trial data against azithromycin or penicillin.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical wound care and keratolytics
medicalTreatments.description: Salicylic acid 5-10% or urea-based creams for plantar hyperkeratosis. Gentle washing and clean dressings of ulcerated papillomata accelerate healing once the spirochete is cleared.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief over 4-8 weeks; adjunctive to systemic antibiotics, not a substitute.

surgicalOptions.name: Reconstructive surgery for gangosa
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for the few remaining adults with childhood-era nasal, palatal, or facial destruction. Local flaps, prosthetics, and palatal obturators may restore function. Should follow confirmed cure with non-treponemal titer fall.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional improvement in 60-80% of selected cases; cosmetic results vary with extent of damage.

whySeeSpecialist: Yaws is increasingly rare and most general clinicians outside endemic countries will never see a case. An infectious disease specialist or tropical medicine physician should evaluate suspected cases to confirm the diagnosis serologically, exclude syphilis (especially in adolescents), and arrange appropriate single-dose therapy. In endemic regions, primary care clinicians and community health workers manage the vast majority of cases under WHO protocols.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic countries, follow your national yaws eradication program's clinic referral pathway — these run regular mass drug administration days. In non-endemic countries, seek a hospital with an infectious diseases or tropical medicine service. Ensure they can perform both treponemal and non-treponemal serology and have access to PCR for confirmation. Travel medicine clinics are also a reasonable starting point for returning travelers with suspicious lesions.
hospitalCriteria: Access to rapid treponemal serology (DPP or equivalent point-of-care test)
Capacity for RPR or VDRL quantitative titer measurement
Stock of azithromycin and benzathine penicillin in pediatric formulations
Trained community health workers participating in mass drug administration campaigns
Link to national surveillance reporting to WHO
keyFacilities: Designated yaws treatment posts in WHO-supported endemic country programs
Tropical medicine and infectious disease departments at regional hospitals
Hospital for Tropical Diseases (London), Bernhard-Nocht-Institut (Hamburg), Antwerp Institute of Tropical Medicine — referral centers for returning travelers
preventionStrategies: Community-wide single-dose azithromycin under the WHO Morges Strategy — the only intervention shown to interrupt transmission at population scale
Improved household access to soap and clean water — reduces incidence by 30-50% even without antibiotic intervention
Treat all household contacts of confirmed cases within 7 days of diagnosis to prevent secondary transmission
Reach 90% population coverage during mass drug administration days to prevent reservoir persistence
Maintain post-MDA surveillance for at least 3 years with rapid response to any new cluster
lifestyleModifications: Wear footwear consistently in endemic areas to reduce plantar inoculation
Treat minor cuts, abrasions, and insect bites promptly with soap and clean water
Avoid sharing bedding, towels, or close skin contact with anyone with an untreated papilloma
Encourage children to attend school-based health screening days where they exist
Report any new persistent skin papilloma to the local health post promptly

recommended: Adequate protein and overall caloric intake to support healing of ulcerated lesions
Vitamin A-rich foods (leafy greens, mango, papaya) which support epithelial healing
Iron and zinc-rich foods to support recovery in children, who often have coexisting deficiencies
Plenty of clean water to support general health and wound healing
avoid: Sharing utensils or unwashed food preparation in households with an active case
Skin contact with unhealed weeping papillomata of others until they have been treated
Untreated drinking water in already malnourished children, who are at higher risk of severe secondary infection

exerciseGuidelines: There are no specific exercise restrictions. Children with painful plantar hyperkeratosis should rest the soles until the lesions soften with treatment, typically 2-4 weeks. Once lesions heal, normal activity resumes without limitation.
dailyManagement: Take or administer the single azithromycin dose under observation when possible to ensure compliance
Keep any ulcerated lesion clean and covered with a simple dressing while it heals
Return for RPR titer check at 6 and 12 months after treatment
Treat any new papilloma promptly with a second course of antibiotics if it appears after initial cure
Report new lesions in household contacts to the local health worker so the cluster can be contained
prognosis: With timely treatment, the prognosis is excellent. A single oral dose of azithromycin cures over 95% of patients, lesions heal within 4-8 weeks, and RPR titers fall fourfold or more in over 90% of cured patients by 6 months. Permanent disfigurement and bone deformity occur only after 5-10 years of untreated infection and are now rare in surveillance-era countries. Sequelae of childhood untreated infection — saber tibia, gangosa, juxta-articular nodes — are essentially irreversible without reconstructive surgery, though active disease and pain resolve with antibiotics at any stage. Mortality from yaws itself is extremely low; the historical burden was almost entirely morbidity from disfigurement, school absence, and stigma. The 2018 emergence of azithromycin-resistant strains in Papua New Guinea is a concern but has not yet derailed eradication efforts, as benzathine penicillin remains universally effective.
recoveryTimeline: Primary lesions begin to flatten within 1-2 weeks of azithromycin and heal completely over 4-8 weeks. Secondary papillomata resolve over similar timeframes. Plantar hyperkeratosis improves over 8-12 weeks. Bone pain settles within 2-4 weeks. RPR titer takes 3-6 months to fall and 12 months to stabilize at the new lower level. Patients are no longer considered infectious within 24 hours of azithromycin or benzathine penicillin.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection of ulcerated papillomata, especially in children with poor wound hygiene
Saber-tibia and saber-shin deformity from chronic periostitis if untreated for years
Gangosa — destructive lesions of the nose, palate, and pharynx in late untreated disease
Juxta-articular subcutaneous nodes that can interfere with joint movement
Chronic skin scarring and pigmentary changes at sites of healed lesions
Stigma-driven school absence and social isolation in children with visible facial or limb lesions

supportResources.name: WHO Yaws Eradication Programme
supportResources.description: Authoritative global program lead, with country dashboards, training materials, and the Morges Strategy operational manuals.

supportResources.name: CDC — Yaws and Endemic Treponematoses
supportResources.description: US public-health reference page useful for travelers and clinicians seeing returning travelers with suspicious lesions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Venereal Syphilis (early)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Syphilis is sexually transmitted, can be congenital, and typically presents with a single painless chancre that heals in 3-6 weeks. Yaws is non-venereal, occurs in young children, presents with a raspberry papilloma rather than a chancre, and is essentially never congenital. Serology cannot distinguish them — age, sexual history, and geography do.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cutaneous TB and lupus vulgaris cause chronic skin ulceration that overlaps clinically with secondary yaws, but TB lesions are typically slow-growing, granulomatous, and accompanied by systemic features. TB serology is negative for treponemal antibodies; Ziehl-Neelsen staining or TB PCR confirms TB.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scabies
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scabies produces intensely itchy papules in skin folds (wrist, finger webs, waistline) caused by Sarcoptes scabiei mite. Yaws lesions are painless or only mildly tender, larger, and concentrated on exposed limbs. Scabies often coexists with yaws in the same endemic communities.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leprosy (Hansen Disease)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leprosy causes hypopigmented anesthetic skin patches and peripheral nerve thickening, neither of which occur in yaws. Lesions show acid-fast bacilli on slit-skin smear. The two diseases can be endemic in the same regions but are distinct on examination.

relatedConditions.name: Venereal Syphilis
relatedConditions.relevance: Closest related infection — caused by a near-identical spirochete subspecies (T. pallidum pallidum) but transmitted sexually and not geographically restricted.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Endemic in the same tropical regions; co-infection is common and complicates skin-lesion differentials.

relatedConditions.name: Leprosy (Hansen Disease)
relatedConditions.relevance: Another disfiguring tropical bacterial infection targeted by WHO neglected-tropical-disease elimination efforts.

relatedConditions.name: Scabies
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists with yaws in the same endemic communities; both transmit by close skin contact and respond to mass drug administration.

faqs.question: Is yaws still around today?
faqs.answer: Yes. Yaws is endemic in 13 countries, with about 46,000 confirmed cases reported globally in 2022 (WHO). Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and several countries in West and Central Africa carry most of the burden. WHO has set 2030 as the target for global eradication; yaws is one of only two human diseases — alongside polio — targeted for total elimination.

faqs.question: How is yaws spread?
faqs.answer: Yaws spreads by direct skin-to-skin contact, usually through minor cuts, scratches, or insect bites. It is not transmitted sexually, not transmitted from mother to baby in the womb, and not transmitted by food, water, or insects acting as vectors. Most transmission happens between children playing or sharing bedding in close contact.

faqs.question: Can adults get yaws?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is uncommon. About 75-80% of new cases occur in children under 15. Adults can acquire yaws but are usually partially protected by immunity from past childhood exposure in endemic communities. In non-endemic countries, travelers can occasionally acquire it after prolonged stays in rural endemic areas.

faqs.question: What does the first lesion of yaws look like?
faqs.answer: The first lesion (the 'mother yaw') is a single, painless, raspberry-like papilloma, typically 1-5 cm wide, on the leg, foot, or buttock. It feels firm, sits on the skin surface like a small fleshy growth, and appears 9-90 days after exposure. It does not hurt unless secondarily infected and heals slowly over months even without treatment.

faqs.question: Is yaws painful?
faqs.answer: The primary papilloma is usually painless. Pain becomes more prominent in secondary disease, particularly from plantar hyperkeratosis (painful sole thickening) and from bone pain in the tibia and forearms. Patients often present with difficulty walking on flat feet, which gave rise to the name 'crab yaws'.

faqs.question: What is the difference between yaws and syphilis?
faqs.answer: Yaws and syphilis are caused by closely related subspecies of the same bacterium (T. pallidum pertenue and T. pallidum pallidum) and are serologically indistinguishable. They differ in transmission and presentation: syphilis is sexually transmitted, can be congenital, and is global; yaws is non-venereal, affects children, and occurs only in tropical regions. Geography and history make the distinction clinically.

faqs.question: How is yaws diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical plus serologic. A characteristic papilloma in a child living in an endemic area plus a positive rapid treponemal test (such as DPP) makes the diagnosis. Quantitative RPR or VDRL titer confirms active disease and monitors treatment. PCR on lesion swabs is the gold standard in surveillance settings.

faqs.question: How is yaws treated?
faqs.answer: A single oral dose of azithromycin at 30 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) cures over 95% of cases. This replaced intramuscular benzathine penicillin as first-line in 2012 because pills are far easier to give at community scale. Penicillin remains the alternative for pregnancy, allergy, or suspected azithromycin resistance.

faqs.question: How quickly does treatment work?
faqs.answer: Patients are no longer infectious within 24 hours of azithromycin or benzathine penicillin. Primary lesions begin to flatten within 1-2 weeks and heal over 4-8 weeks. Bone pain settles within 2-4 weeks. Antibody titers (RPR) take 3-6 months to fall fourfold, which is the laboratory marker of cure.

faqs.question: Can yaws come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse is rare with adequate treatment but does occur in 1-3% of cases, usually within 6 months. Re-treatment with a second dose of azithromycin, or switching to benzathine penicillin, cures most relapses. Persistent failure should prompt PCR testing for the 23S rRNA macrolide-resistance mutation.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for yaws?
faqs.answer: No vaccine for yaws is currently available. Eradication relies on mass drug administration with azithromycin, contact tracing, and improved community hygiene. A vaccine has been technically feasible but not prioritized because oral antibiotic therapy is already highly effective.

faqs.question: Where does yaws still occur?
faqs.answer: WHO listed 13 endemic countries in its 2022 status report: Cameroon, Central African Republic, Republic of the Congo, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Indonesia, Liberia, Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, Timor-Leste, Togo, and Vanuatu. Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands together account for over 70% of reported cases.

faqs.question: Why is yaws being eradicated, not just controlled?
faqs.answer: Yaws affects only humans, has no animal reservoir, has a sensitive single-dose oral treatment, and is geographically confined. These four features make it biologically eradicable — similar to smallpox and polio. WHO set 2030 as the eradication target, though progress has been slowed by funding gaps and the 2018 resistance outbreak.

faqs.question: What is gangosa?
faqs.answer: Gangosa is the destructive late-stage facial lesion of untreated tertiary yaws, eroding the nose, palate, and pharynx. It develops 5-10 years after primary infection. Modern surveillance has essentially eliminated new gangosa; most living cases reflect childhood infections from before the 1970s eradication era.

faqs.question: Can yaws spread through sexual contact?
faqs.answer: No. Yaws is not a sexually transmitted infection. Although it is caused by a close relative of the syphilis bacterium, sexual transmission has not been demonstrated for T. pertenue. Adolescent cases with positive treponemal serology require careful history-taking to distinguish yaws from acquired syphilis.

faqs.question: Is yaws fatal?
faqs.answer: Yaws itself is rarely fatal. The historical burden has been disfigurement, school absence, and stigma rather than mortality. Death from yaws occurs only in extreme tertiary cases with secondary bacterial sepsis or in severely malnourished children with overwhelming co-infection.

faqs.question: What does azithromycin resistance in yaws mean?
faqs.answer: A 2018 outbreak in Papua New Guinea identified yaws strains with the A2058G mutation in 23S rRNA, conferring high-level azithromycin resistance. This is a concern for the eradication program. Benzathine penicillin remains universally effective against resistant strains, and resistance has not yet spread widely outside the original outbreak focus.

faqs.question: Should travelers worry about yaws?
faqs.answer: Risk to short-term travelers is very low. Yaws requires repeated close skin contact, typically over weeks to months, in rural endemic communities. Travelers to endemic areas should treat any persistent unhealed skin papilloma with a tropical medicine clinician on return. Standard travel vaccinations do not cover yaws because none exist.

faqs.question: How is mass drug administration for yaws organized?
faqs.answer: WHO's Morges Strategy involves total community treatment — every resident, regardless of symptoms, receives a single oral dose of azithromycin on a designated day. Coverage above 90% is the target. Re-survey at 6-12 month intervals identifies new cases, who are then re-treated along with their household contacts. The cycle continues until no new cases for 3 consecutive years.

faqs.question: Can yaws be prevented without antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Partial prevention is possible through improved hygiene — handwashing, soap availability, footwear, prompt cleaning of cuts and insect bites — which reduces incidence by 30-50%. However, hygiene alone has not eliminated yaws anywhere historically. Community-wide single-dose azithromycin is the only intervention that has interrupted transmission at population scale.

simpleName: Yaws (tropical bacterial skin and bone infection)
metaDescription: Yaws is a tropical skin and bone infection in children, curable with one azithromycin dose. Symptoms, diagnosis, and WHO eradication strategy.

images.caption: Secondary yaws — multiple raspberry-like papillomata in a child from an endemic region.

images.altText: Mother yaw primary papilloma on the lower leg showing the characteristic raspberry surface texture
images.caption: Primary yaws lesion (mother yaw) on the lower leg of a child.

sources.title: WHO. Eradication of yaws — the Morges Strategy. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2012, updated 2020.

Mitja O, Hays R, Ipai A, et al. Single-dose azithromycin versus benzathine benzylpenicillin for treatment of yaws in children in Papua New Guinea: an open-label, non-inferiority, randomised trial. Lancet. 2012;379(9813):342-347.
